content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
October 7,
1994
Gregory Taylor
Metropolitan Water District
of Southern California
350 South Grand Avenue
Los Angeles,
CA
90071
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1—94—289
Dear Mr. Taylor:
You have requested informal assistance concerning new
“ethics” provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”)
1 which
further limit the receipt of gifts and honoraria by specified
public officials effective January 1,
1995.2
QUESTION
Will existing regulations under the Act concerning gift and
honoraria limits,
including travel,
apply to members of governing
boards of special districts and designated employees of local
agencies under the new law?
CONCLUSION
Yes.
Existing regulations under the Act concerning gift and
honoraria limits, including travel, will apply to members of
governing boards of special districts and designated employees of
local agencies under the new law.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000
- 18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—94—289
Page
2
To the extent that other laws outside the Political Reform
Act may further limit reimbursement for actual travel and
reasonable subsistence, those laws would apply.
MTALYSIS
You ask fqr general guidance concerning the application of a
new ethics law (Chapter 36, Stats.
1994), to be effective
January 1,
1995.
As amended, Section 89501 provides:
(a)
No local elected officeholders, elected
or appointed members of the governing board of a
special district, or designated employee of a local
government agency shall accept any honorarium, as
defined in subdivisions
(b),
(c),
and (e)
of
Section 89502.
(b)
No local elected officeholder, elected or
appointed member of the governing board of a
special district, or designated employee of a local
government agency shall accept any gifts,
from any
single source, which is in excess of two hundred
fifty dollars ($250), in any calendar year except
reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
The commission shall adjust this gift limitation to
make it equal to the prevailing gift limitation
amount applicable to elected state officers in
effect on January 1,
1995,
and thereafter shall
adjust this gift limit at the same time and in the
same amount as the gift limit applicable to elected
state officers is adjuste4 pursuant to subdivision
(d)
of Section 89504.
Thus, the new law will prohibit the specified public
officials from accepting y honorarium for any speech given or
article published, or attendnce at any public or private
conference, convention, meeting, social event, meal, or like
gathering.
(Section 89501(a) , as amended, .and existing Section
89502(b).)
In addition, these public officials will also be
prohibited from accepting gifts in excess of $270 in any
calendar year, except reimbursement for actual travel expenses and
reasonable subsistence in connection therewith.
(Section
89501(b),
as amended.)
Gifts are reportable and may subject a public official to
disqualification.
(Sections 87027 and 87103.)
The adjusted gift limit applicable to elected state officers
until January 1,
1995 is $270.
It will be adjusted biennially by
the Commission and,
in 1995, is likely to further increase from
the original gift limit amount of $250.
(Regulation 18954.)


Our File No.
1—94—289
Page
3
It appears that the general intent of the legislation was to
subject local and appointed elected officials and designated
employees of local government agencies to the same gift and
honoraria limits generally applicable to state officials.
5
Pursuant to current law, elected state officers, members of
state boards and commissions,
and designated employees of state
agencies are prohibited from accepting any honorarium,
and they
are subject to a $270 gift limit from a single source in any
calendar year.
(Sections 89502 to 89506.)
In addition, existing
Section 89501 subjects local elected officeholders to a combined
gift and honoraria limit of $1,000 in any calendar year from any
single source.
This would include elected members of special
districts,
who are “local elected officeholders” covered under
existing law, but who are specifically mentioned in the new
statute.
Therefore, existing Commission regulations concerning
gifts, honoraria,
and travel which are applicable to these
officials include Regulations 18930 to 18935 and Regulations 18950
to 18950.4
(copies enclosed).
The general question you pose is whether existing Commission
regulations concerning gift and honoraria limits,
and travel,
apply to the additional public officials (appointed members of
a governing board of a special district and designated employees
of a local government agency)
subject to the new law.
Gifts and Honoraria, Generally
The amendments to Section 89501 expressly incorporate the
definition of the term “honorarium,” as defined in subdivision
(b)
of Section 89502,
as well as applicable exclusions and a provision
for return of honorarium under subdivisions
(b),
(c),
and (e).
Section 89502 provides in pertinent part:
(b)
“Honorarium” means, except as provided in
subdivision (c),
any paTment made in consideration
for any speech given, article published,
or
attendance at any public or private conference,
convention, meeting, social event, meal,
or like
gathering.
(c)
“Honorarium” does not include earned
income for personal services which are customarily
provided in connection with the practice of a bona
fide business, trade, or profession,
such as
teaching, practicing law, medicine,
insurance, real
estate,
banking,
or building contracting, unless
g,
for example, the Senate Local Government Committee
Analysis on AB 1542
(Umberg) dated July 1,
1993.


Our File No.
1—94—289
Page
4
the sole or predominant activity of the business,
trade, or profession is making speeches....
(d)
Section 89506 shall apply to all
payments,
advances, reimbursements for travel and
related lodging and subsistence.
(e)
This section does not apply to any
honorarium which is not used,
and within 30 days
after receipt, is either returned to the donor or
delivered to the Controller for donation to the
General Fund without being claimed as a deduction
from income for tax purposes.
In addition, the existing applicable definition of the term
“gift” pursuant to Section 82028 has not been amended.
Therefore,
existing Commission gifts and honoraria regulations, which may be
applicable, will generally apply to public officials subject to
the new law.
(Hanna Advice Letter,
No.
1-94—243.)
Travel
You specifically ask whether the Commission’s travel
regulations will apply.
We believe it is consistent with the
existing interpretation of the Act and the new law to apply the
applicable travel regulations as discussed below.
With respect to travel exemptions from the gift ‘imit,
generally, Section 89506, has been applied to local elected
officials in Regulation 18950.2.
However, the new statute does
not cross-reference existing Section 89502 (d), which provides that
Section 89506 applies to all payments,
advances, reimbursements
for travel and related lodging and subsistence.
In adopting Regulation 18950.2, the Commission has previously
incorporated in its regulatory framework the substance of the
exceptions set forth in Section 89506.6
This would also be true
of Regulation 18950.3, which incorporated the substance of long
standing exemptions regarding —necessary acóommodations and
intrastate travel which are directly in connection with a speech,
panel,
or seminar.
(Former Regulation 18728(a).)
As a general rule of statutory construction, it must be
presumed that a practical administrative construction was brought
to the attention of the Legislature and that the amendment of a
statute, the meaning of which is well-established by
6
See staff memorandum to the Commission, Final Discussion of
Travel and Related Regulations
-- Adoption of Regulations 18950,
18950.1,
18950.2.
18950.3,
18950.4
(Former 18228’) ; Repeal of
Regulations 18623,
18728 dated March 27,
1992.
Regulation 18950.2
placed into the regulatory framework exemptions stemming from
Sections 89501 and 89506.


Our File No.
1-94-289
Page 5
administrative construction, constitutes legislative confirmation
or adoption of such construction.
e.g.,
El Dorado Oil Works
v. McColgan
(1950)
34 C.2d 731.)
Thus,
we conclude it is
consistent with the existing interpretation of the Act and the new
legislation to apply Regulations 18950.2 and 18950.3 to the
officials covered under the new law.
7
However, to the extent that other laws outside of the
jurisdiction of the Commission may further limit reimbursement for
actual travel and reasonable subsistence, those laws would apply.
For example,
you ask about the definition of “reimbursement” and
“individual charge” as used in new Section 53065.5 of the
Government Code.
This new statute is not under the jurisdiction
of the Commission.
I trust this letter has addressed your concerns.
Should you
have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free
to contact me at
(916)
322_5901,.8
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division.
Please note,
however, that it will be necessary for the
Commission to make necessary technical amendments to the
regulations to conform to the language of the new statute.
8
Copies of Commission regulations and opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of l0 per page.

honoraria limits,
including travel,
apply to members of governing
boards of special districts and designated employees of local
agencies under the new law?

CONCLUSION: Yes.
Existing regulations under the Act concerning gift and
honoraria limits, including travel, will apply to members of
governing boards of special districts and designated employees of
local agencies under the new law.
;
2 Cal.
Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c) (3).)
